title: Day 1 · Phase 1 — Overview, safety zones, theme commit
bodyHtml:
DAY 1 · Phase 1 — Overview, safety, theme

Walk the corridor; meet the four hazard zones; commit a team theme.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS (preview)

K1. Four components of a DC circuit. K2. Energy chain preview. K4. Veer cause preview.

Lesson Flow

(~50 min; adjust for block.)

TimeWhat happens
0–5Hand out packets. Frame the unit: "For 9 days you'll build a car. Day 9 you race it AND write a transfer task defending three physics choices."
5–15Walk the actual race corridor. Students stand in it. Notice the width. Notice what a 2-cm veer does over 6 m. The corridor IS the design constraint.
15–25Vocabulary preview (battery, switch, motor, series, current) — named, not defined yet. Project the parts kit.
25–40Four safety zones: soldering iron, hot glue, drill, switch installation. Show, don't explain. Each subsequent day opens with a 60-second beat on the relevant zone.
40–48Team formation (3–4 per team) + theme commitment. Teams write theme + biggest expected physical challenge on packet exit ticket.
48–50Marzano 1-2-3 conversation on K1/K2/K4 preview.

Prep: packets printed; corridor cleared; one parts kit per team staged on tables; safety reference page open.

Differentiation: ELs — pair theme commitments with a sentence frame; SWDs — give team-role assignment cards (build lead, safety, scribe).

Formative check: exit-ticket completeness; theme clear enough for a stranger to recognize.

title: Day 2 · Phase 1 — PhET circuit + soldering practice
bodyHtml:
DAY 2 · Phase 1 — Simulate it, then practice the iron

Build a working series circuit in PhET; produce a clean scrap solder joint.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K1. Four DC components. K2. Energy chain. S1. Series circuit in PhET. S2. Solder on scrap.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–1Safety beat: soldering iron.
1–15PhET Circuit Construction Kit: DC. Teacher demos the loop (battery → switch → bulb stand-in for motor). Students build it. Probe: open the switch, double the voltage, break a wire. Three observations recorded.
15–25Energy-chain worked example: chemical → electrical → mechanical-rotational → mechanical-translational. Students label each arrow on their packet diagram.
25–40Scrap solder practice. Two joints turned in. Teacher inspects: shiny, tight, compact.
40–48Marzano conversation. Quick sketch of the four-link energy chain in their own words.
48–50Tomorrow: gears, wheels, ratio reasoning, wheel commit.

Prep: PhET loaded on lab computers (offline build is fine); 1 iron per pod with stand; scrap wire + solder; eye protection.

Talk moves: on first power in PhET — "What did you predict would happen? What did happen?" Make the model-vs-observation move habitual now; it pays off on Day 6.

Differentiation: finished early → solder a third joint with stranded wire (harder); behind → pair with finished team for inspection round.

title: Day 3 · Phase 1 — Mechanical advantage + wheel commit
bodyHtml:
DAY 3 · Phase 1 — The one design decision

Wheel selection: ratio reasoning drives the prediction.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K3. Gear-pair RPM/torque relationship. R1. Ratio reasoning for wheel diameter. R2. Justify wheel choice.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–1Safety beat: hot glue (tomorrow).
1–10CALLOUT: "The motor only spins. Gears change how." Vocab pass: RPM, gear ratio, torque, circumference, mechanical advantage.
10–22Worked example 1: 2:1 gear ratio (10-tooth on motor, 20-tooth on axle). Worked example 2: wheel circumference C = π × d.
22–38Wheel comparison table. Teams measure each candidate wheel pair, compute C, predict relative speed and starting effort vs. the smallest wheel. Ratio reasoning rule: doubling diameter doubles top speed at the same axle RPM AND doubles starting effort.
38–46Wheel commit. Teams write: "We chose ___-mm wheels because ___; we predict our top speed will be ___ relative to a team that chose ___ because ___."
46–50Marzano on K3/R1/R2. Tomorrow: chassis cut.

Prep: sample wheels of each available size at the front; rulers; the 10T motor gear + 20T axle gear shown side by side as the canonical pair.

Talk moves: when students hedge ("we'll just see what works") — "Make a prediction. Day 6 will tell you whether you were right. That's the whole point."

Differentiation: ELs — sentence frame for the prediction; advanced — challenge to estimate ABSOLUTE top speed (they'll need to assume a motor RPM, which is the lesson on Day 6).

title: Day 4 · Phase 2 — Theme + chassis cut (Build steps 1–3)
bodyHtml:
DAY 4 · Phase 2 — Build begins

Chassis cut; axle line drawn in pencil BEFORE anything else.

TODAY'S DELIVERABLE

Balsa chassis cut to spec, with both intended axle positions marked as a single straight pencil line across the chassis.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–1Safety beat: hot glue (you'll see it tomorrow).
1–5Confirm theme. Confirm wheel set in kit.
5–15Mini-lecture on the axle line: "If your axles aren't on a straight line on Day 5, your car cannot roll straight on Day 6. Day 5 depends on Day 4."
15–45Chassis cut + axle line drawn. Teacher circulates, checking the axle line is one straight pencil mark across, not two squiggles.
45–50Marzano conversation on cumulative targets. Pencil down.

Prep: balsa sheets on tables, rulers, pencils, X-acto knives + cutting mats, masks for dust.

Differentiation: ahead → start sketching theme finish on chassis underside; behind → pair with finished team to mark axle line first.

Formative check: hold up the chassis at eye level: is the axle line ONE straight line? If not, redraw before anyone leaves.

title: Day 5 · Phase 2 — Axles + alignment (Build steps 4–9)
bodyHtml:
DAY 5 · Phase 2 — The day that decides the race

Axles parallel. Roll-straight ≥3 m unpowered.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K4. Veer cause = friction asymmetry → vertical-axis torque. S4. Parallel axle install.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–1Safety beat: hot glue.
1–10CALLOUT: "Why parallel matters." Top-down sketch of friction asymmetry → torque about a vertical axis through the car. Not a power problem. A geometry problem.
10–35Build steps 4–9: pen-tube axle holders, drill 3/16" holes along the Day-4 line, slide axles, press-fit wheels, mount the 20T axle gear (slightly offset for motor mesh tomorrow). Roll test #1.
35–45Adjust. Re-glue. Roll test #2. Data table records distance before veer + what changed.
45–50Marzano on K4/S4. Teams that did NOT hit 3 m straight: record what they'll adjust before tomorrow's first power-on.

Prep: pen tubes pre-cut; 3/16" bit; hot glue guns at glue station; corridor available for roll tests.

Talk moves: when a car veers — "Which wheel has more friction? How do you know? What about the geometry would cause that?" Push for the physical cause before the fix.

Differentiation: ahead → help neighbor teams with their roll tests (peer-teach K4); behind → teacher does the first axle-holder glue WITH the team to model precision.

title: Day 6 · Phase 2 — Gears, motor, switch, FIRST POWER + RPM (Build steps 10–15)
bodyHtml:
DAY 6 · Phase 2 — First power-on + measurement

Wire the loop. Power on. Measure axle RPM. Compare to Day 3 prediction.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K1/K2 reinforced. S5. Switch install without breaking chassis. S6. Measure axle RPM. R1 revisited.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–1Safety beat: drill + first power-on.
1–25Build steps 10–15: motor gear, motor mount + mesh, drill switch hole (CHASSIS-BREAK WATCH — wood block under chassis), battery clip, wire battery → switch → motor in series (tape splices for now), direction test.
25–30Mini-lecture beat: name the wires they just connected. Battery = source. Switch = gate. Motor = load. "Break the loop anywhere, current stops everywhere."
30–42RPM measurement protocol. Mark 2.00 m. Switch on at start, stopwatch starts. Stop at finish. Compute v = d/t, rev/s = v/C, RPM = (rev/s) × 60. Three runs.
42–48Comparison cell: predicted Day-3 speed vs. measured Day-6 speed. 1–2 sentence reflection on prediction-vs-observation.
48–50Marzano on S5/S6.

Prep: drills with 3/16" bits + wood blocks; tape; stopwatches (or phones); 2-meter tape on the floor; corridor.

Talk moves on first power-on: teacher prompt before the click — "What direction will the car go? Why?" Right after the click — "What surprised you? What didn't?" Make it the modeling conversation, not a celebration.

Differentiation: ahead → run a second RPM trial with the switch held down only briefly (energy depletion check); behind → teacher pairs to hold stopwatch + record while team focuses on driving.

title: Day 7 · Phase 3 — Solder commits + aesthetic finish begins (Build steps 16–20)
bodyHtml:
DAY 7 · Phase 3 — Lock in + dress up

Solder every splice. Final alignment recheck. Paint base on.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S3. Clean solder joints on actual car wiring. S7. Theme execution begins.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–1Safety beat: soldering iron, second take.
1–25Solder every wire splice. Tape comes off. Hot-glue loose wires so they cannot snag.
25–32Final alignment roll test. Confirm the car still rolls straight ≥3 m unpowered. If alignment broke during soldering, fix now.
32–46Aesthetic finish begins: base coat, primary decoration mounting prep. Theme commitment from Day 1 is the rubric.
46–50Marzano on S3.

Prep: irons + solder + heat shrink; paint + brushes; decoration common pool; classroom newspaper to cover paint tables.

Talk moves: when a team rushes the finish — "Day 8 design judging is theme execution + finish craftsmanship + build craftsmanship. What do you want the judge to see?"

Differentiation: ahead → start practice runs in the corridor with their car (early Day 8 work); behind → solder the most critical splice first, tape the rest as fallback.

title: Day 8 · Phase 3 — Finish, signature, photo, practice (Build steps 19–23)
bodyHtml:
DAY 8 · Phase 3 — Race-ready

Theme complete. Practice heats. Tomorrow is the race.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S7. Theme execution complete.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–1Safety beat: choose the most relevant zone for the day's work.
1–20Decoration finish, paint touch-ups, sealant if needed.
20–28Sign the underside of the chassis with team name + date. Write the name card for tomorrow's judging table.
28–35Group photoshoot (cars side by side, then individual hero shots).
35–48Practice runs in the actual corridor. Record practice times. Tune.
48–50Marzano on S7. Tomorrow: race + judging + transfer task.

Prep: camera/phone; name cards; corridor cleared for practice; lap timer (or phone).

Talk moves on design judging conversation: when teams ask what scores well — "Three things: did you honor your Day-1 theme commitment? Is the finish craftsmanship visible? Is the build craftsmanship visible? Theme + finish + build. The race is its own conversation."

Differentiation: ahead → run a competitor's car too and offer feedback; behind → set a single specific tuning goal (e.g., "reduce veer") and use practice runs to test it.

title: Day 9 · Phase 4 — Race + design judging + transfer task (Build step 24)
bodyHtml:
DAY 9 · Phase 4 — The transfer day

Race in heats. Judge design. Defend three physics choices in writing.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS (load-bearing)

R3. Observation vs. prediction. R4. Three physics choices defended. Final ratings recorded for ALL unit targets.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–3Race format briefed. Heats called. Rubric posted: time + straightness (1-2-3); theme + finish + build (1-2-3).
3–22Heats. Teacher records time + straightness as each heat runs. Photos.
22–32Design judging at the table. Teacher (optional peer voting) scores theme execution, finish craftsmanship, build craftsmanship.
32–48Transfer task. Three prompts in the packet — wheels (with computed speed), alignment, circuit + counterfactual. Rubric is 4-dimension (Identify / Calculate / Construct / Transfer), lowest dimension wins. Letter scale.
48–50Final Marzano conversation. Growth chart completed across the unit.

Prep: race corridor marked + cleared; stopwatch; judging table with one slot per team; signed-chassis check before heats.

Talk moves on first race surprise: when a car wins that wasn't expected — "Why did this car win? Reason from the physics, not the team." Make the race a thinking artifact, not a competition.

Differentiation on the transfer task: ELs — sentence frames for each of the 3 prompts; SWDs — verbal version of the wheel computation, written version of the other two; ahead → add a 4th prompt on what they'd change next time and why.

Closing: growth chart completed across the unit's 15 targets. Marzano conversation closes the unit and the CPA year.
